summary: Reporter describes a ~90% performance regression when drawing a rectangle with a linear gradient shader in SkiaSharp 3.116.0 compared to 2.88.9 on Windows, where simply commenting out the shader restores performance.

stepsToReproduce: Create an SKPaint with SKShader.CreateLinearGradient (2 colors, DarkBlue to Black)
Call canvas.DrawRect with the gradient paint in a PaintSurface handler
Observe significantly slower rendering compared to v2.88.9
Comment out the Shader assignment to confirm it returns to normal speed

bugSignals.errorMessage: PaintSurface becomes very slow when using a linear gradient shader

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The gradient shader code path in SkiaSharp is a thin wrapper around Skia's SkGradientShader::MakeLinear. Performance differences are likely due to the upstream Skia version change between v2 and v3, which has not been reverted.

regression.reason: Reporter explicitly states gradient rendering was fast in v2.88.9 and became very slow in v3.116.0+. Multiple similar performance regression reports exist across major version bumps (#731, #543, #3525).

analysis.summary: Performance regression in linear gradient shader rendering between SkiaSharp v2 (Skia m88) and v3 (newer Skia). The C# and C API wrappers are thin pass-throughs — the bottleneck is in Skia's upstream SkGradientShader::MakeLinear or the raster backend's gradient fill pipeline. The reporter's code pattern (reusing a single SKPaint with shader, calling DrawRect repeatedly in PaintSurface) is standard usage. This is part of a broader pattern of performance regressions across major Skia version bumps.
analysis.rationale: Classified as type/bug because the reporter describes a clear performance regression between versions with identical code. The C# wrapper at SKShader.cs:125-137 and C API at sk_shader.cpp:63-68 are trivially thin — there is no SkiaSharp-specific logic that could cause a 90% slowdown. The regression is almost certainly in the upstream Skia raster pipeline. Area is area/SkiaSharp (core shader) rather than Views because the slowdown is in the drawing operation itself, not the view event infrastructure.

keySignals.text: Having a gradient background was no issue with Version="2.88.9". Using latest one or even the preview (3.118), the performance is down by 90%.
keySignals.interpretation: Clear regression claim with specific version boundaries. v2→v3 is a major Skia upstream upgrade.

keySignals.text: Simply commenting out the Shader block, it returns fast... but flat.
keySignals.interpretation: Confirms the shader itself is the bottleneck — not DrawRect, not surface creation, not the view infrastructure.
Suggests an animated or game-like scenario where PaintSurface is called at high frequency, amplifying any per-frame performance regression.

codeInvestigation.finding: CreateLinearGradient is a thin wrapper: validates args, pins arrays, calls SkiaApi.sk_shader_new_linear_gradient. No allocation, copy, or transformation overhead in the C# layer that could explain a 90% slowdown.
C API calls SkGradientShader::MakeLinear directly. The only overhead is an optional matrix conversion. The performance difference must originate in the upstream Skia implementation.
DrawRect is a simple P/Invoke to sk_canvas_draw_rect with null check — no additional overhead.

nextQuestions: Is the reporter using SKCanvasView (raster) or SKGLView (GPU)? Raster gradients are more affected by Skia upstream changes.
What are the dimensions of the gradient fill? Full-screen raster gradient fills are CPU-intensive.
How frequently is PaintSurface called? An animation loop would amplify per-frame overhead.
Does GPU-accelerated rendering (SKGLView) eliminate the performance gap?

resolution.hypothesis: The upstream Skia raster pipeline changed gradient rendering between the versions used in SkiaSharp v2 (m88) and v3 (newer milestone), resulting in significantly slower CPU-rendered gradients. This is not a SkiaSharp wrapper issue but an upstream Skia behavior change.

proposals.title: Switch to GPU-accelerated rendering
proposals.description: If using SKCanvasView (CPU/raster), switching to SKGLView (GPU-accelerated via OpenGL) should significantly improve gradient rendering performance. GPU shaders handle gradients natively and bypass the raster pipeline bottleneck entirely.

proposals.title: Cache gradient as bitmap
proposals.description: Pre-render the gradient to an SKBitmap once, then draw the bitmap in PaintSurface instead of applying the shader every frame. This avoids re-evaluating the gradient shader per-pixel on every frame.

proposals.title: Profile Skia raster gradient pipeline
proposals.description: Profile the raster gradient fill path in the current Skia version to identify the specific bottleneck. Compare with the Skia version used in SkiaSharp 2.88.x to quantify the upstream regression.

resolution.recommendedReason: Immediately actionable by the reporter, avoids the per-frame shader evaluation cost regardless of rendering backend, and requires minimal code changes. The GPU switch is also good but requires more structural changes and may not be available in all deployment targets.

actionability.reason: Genuine performance regression with plausible root cause in upstream Skia. Needs profiling to confirm whether it's a raster-specific issue or affects GPU rendering too. Reporter's code pattern is standard — this is not misuse.

actions.description: Apply bug, performance, and platform labels
Acknowledge regression, suggest bitmap caching workaround, ask clarifying questions
Cross-reference to broader SkiaSharp v3 performance regression report

missingInfo: Whether the reporter is using SKCanvasView (CPU) or SKGLView (GPU)
Approximate dimensions of the gradient-filled rectangle
How frequently PaintSurface is invoked (timer-based animation? user interaction?)
